module.title: CONTROL & RANGES
module.subtitle: if/unless · case · ranges · times · while
module.theory:
## Branching: if / unless
Ruby chooses a path with **if**. The opposite of `if` is **unless** — it runs when the condition is **false**. Both end with **end**.
~~~ruby
if power > 50
  "online"
elsif power > 0
  "low"
else
  "dead"
end
~~~
Because `if` is an **expression**, the whole block has a value — you can return it or assign it:
~~~ruby
status = if power > 0 then "on" else "off" end
~~~

## case / when
When you compare one value against many options, **case** is cleaner than a stack of `elsif`. Each **when** can list several values.
~~~ruby
case code
when 200 then "ok"
when 404 then "missing"
when 500, 502, 503 then "server down"
else "unknown"
end
~~~

## Ranges: 1..n and 1...n
A **range** is a span of values. Two dots **1..5** is *inclusive* (1,2,3,4,5). Three dots **1...5** is *exclusive* — it stops one short (1,2,3,4). Turn one into an array with **.to_a**.
~~~ruby
(1..5).to_a    # [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
(1...5).to_a   # [1, 2, 3, 4]
(1..5).include?(5)   # true
(1...5).include?(5)  # false
~~~

> INTEL — Count the dots. Two dots reaches the end; three dots leaves it behind. The classic off-by-one bug is using `...` when you meant `..`.

## Looping without a counter: times / upto / while
You rarely write a manual counter in Ruby. **n.times** runs a block n times (with index 0..n-1). **a.upto(b)** counts from a up to b inclusive. **while** repeats as long as a condition holds.
~~~ruby
sum = 0
3.times { |i| sum += i }   # i = 0,1,2  -> sum 3

total = 0
1.upto(4) { |n| total += n }  # 1+2+3+4 -> 10

n = 10
while n > 1
  n = n / 2
end
~~~

> WARNING — `3.times` gives you indexes **0, 1, 2** — not 1, 2, 3. If you need 1..3, use `1.upto(3)` instead.

## The ternary: cond ? a : b
A one-line `if/else`. Read it as "condition? then a, else b".
~~~ruby
label = score >= 60 ? "pass" : "fail"
~~~

## Guard clauses: return early
Rather than wrapping the whole method in a big `if`, **bail out early** with a one-line guard. `return value if condition` reads left-to-right and keeps the happy path un-indented.
~~~ruby
def reciprocal(x)
  return 0 if x == 0
  1.0 / x
end
~~~

> INTEL — `return x if cond` and `puts x unless cond` are *statement modifiers*: the action comes first, the condition trails. They only make sense for a single line.

exercises.title: GROUND THE SIGNAL
exercises.prompt:
Define a method **clamp_zero(n)** that returns `n` when it is zero or positive, but returns **0** when `n` is negative.
~~~ruby
clamp_zero(7)   # 7
clamp_zero(-3)  # 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
def clamp_zero(n)
  # TODO: negatives should become 0
  n
end
exercises.solution:
def clamp_zero(n)
  if n < 0
    0
  else
    n
  end
end
exercises.lore: Drain the negative voltage to earth.

exercises.title: ACCESS GATE
exercises.prompt:
Define **access(level)** that returns `"GRANTED"` only when `level` is **5 or higher**, otherwise `"DENIED"`. Use **unless** (deny *unless* the level is high enough) or a normal if — your call.
~~~ruby
access(5)  # "GRANTED"
access(2)  # "DENIED"
~~~
exercises.solution:
def access(level)
  return "DENIED" unless level >= 5
  "GRANTED"
end

exercises.title: STATUS DECODER
exercises.prompt:
Define **status_text(code)** using **case ... when** that returns:
- `"OK"` for **200**
- `"NOT FOUND"` for **404**
- `"SERVER ERROR"` for **500, 502 or 503**
- `"UNKNOWN"` for anything else
~~~ruby
status_text(502)  # "SERVER ERROR"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def status_text(code)
  case code
  when 200 then "OK"
  # TODO: handle 404 and the server errors
  else "UNKNOWN"
  end
end
exercises.solution:
def status_text(code)
  case code
  when 200 then "OK"
  when 404 then "NOT FOUND"
  when 500, 502, 503 then "SERVER ERROR"
  else "UNKNOWN"
  end
end
exercises.lore: The relay answers in numbers. Translate.

exercises.title: RANGE TOTAL
exercises.prompt:
Define **range_sum(n)** that returns the sum of every integer from **1 to n inclusive**. Build the inclusive range with **(1..n)** and use **.sum**.
~~~ruby
range_sum(5)  # 15  (1+2+3+4+5)
~~~
exercises.starter:
def range_sum(n)
  # TODO: sum 1..n inclusive (watch the dots!)
  (1...n).sum
end
exercises.lore: Tally every node on the bus.

exercises.title: LAUNCH COUNTDOWN
exercises.prompt:
Define **countdown(n)** that returns an **array** counting down from `n` to `1`, with the string `"LIFTOFF"` appended at the end. Use **n.downto(1)** to collect the numbers.
~~~ruby
countdown(3)  # [3, 2, 1, "LIFTOFF"]
~~~
exercises.starter:
def countdown(n)
  out = []
  # TODO: push n, n-1, ... 1 then "LIFTOFF"
  out
end

exercises.title: VERDICT
exercises.brief: Pass or fail in one line.
exercises.prompt:
Define **verdict(score)** that returns `"PASS"` when `score` is **60 or more**, otherwise `"FAIL"`. Write it as a **single ternary expression**: `cond ? a : b`.
~~~ruby
verdict(72)  # "PASS"
verdict(40)  # "FAIL"
~~~
exercises.starter:
def verdict(score)
  # TODO: use the ternary  cond ? "PASS" : "FAIL"
  "FAIL"
end
exercises.lore: The tribunal decides in a single breath.

exercises.title: HALVING PROTOCOL
exercises.prompt:
Define **halving_steps(n)** that repeatedly does integer-division by 2 until `n` reaches **1**, and returns **how many halvings** it took. Use a **while** loop. Assume `n >= 1`.
~~~ruby
halving_steps(8)  # 3   (8 -> 4 -> 2 -> 1)
halving_steps(1)  # 0
~~~
exercises.starter:
def halving_steps(n)
  steps = 0
  # TODO: while n > 1, halve n and count
  steps
end
exercises.solution:
def halving_steps(n)
  steps = 0
  while n > 1
    n = n / 2
    steps += 1
  end
  steps
end
exercises.lore: Fold the signal in half until only the seed remains.

exercises.title: PULSE GRID
exercises.prompt:
Define **pulse_grid(n)** returning an **array** of length `n`. For each number from **1 to n inclusive**, the entry is:
- `"FIZZBUZZ"` if divisible by both 3 and 5
- `"FIZZ"` if divisible by 3
- `"BUZZ"` if divisible by 5
- otherwise the **number itself**

Use **1.upto(n)** (or **(1..n)**) and check the both-case first.
~~~ruby
pulse_grid(5)  # [1, 2, "FIZZ", 4, "BUZZ"]
~~~
exercises.starter:
def pulse_grid(n)
  out = []
  1.upto(n) do |i|
    # TODO: FIZZBUZZ / FIZZ / BUZZ / i
    out << i
  end
  out
end
exercises.solution:
def pulse_grid(n)
  out = []
  1.upto(n) do |i|
    if i % 3 == 0 && i % 5 == 0
      out << "FIZZBUZZ"
    elsif i % 3 == 0
      out << "FIZZ"
    elsif i % 5 == 0
      out << "BUZZ"
    else
      out << i
    end
  end
  out
end
exercises.hint: Test i % 3 == 0 && i % 5 == 0 BEFORE the single checks.
exercises.lore: Every third beat flares. Every fifth one howls.
